title: Jesus Christ—The Final Revelation, Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 1-12-25 -PM- Jesus Christ-The Final Revelation - Part 1 - Hebrews 1_1-2 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Context and Purpose of Hebrews
subpoints: The author of Hebrews was a Bible-saturated, brethren-loving, word-applying pastor
The letter was likely written before AD 70 to discourage Jewish Christians from returning to Judaism
The audience had made a profession of faith but were tempted by familiarity with Old Testament ritual
The pastor's core argument: Christ is the ultimate 'better'—nothing is superior to Him

outline.title: God's Final Word Is Supremely Authoritative
subpoints: God spoke in many ways through the prophets, but has now spoken finally through His Son
Christ is not merely a greater revelation but the final and complete revelation of God
F.F. Bruce: 'The story of divine revelation is a story of progression up to Christ. But there is no progression beyond Him.'
In Luke 24, Jesus showed the disciples how all of Scripture pointed to Himself
The phrase 'in these last days' signals the eschatological finality of Christ's revelation

outline.title: Application 1: Christ Is the Only Truth in a Pluralistic Age
subpoints: Our culture rejects the idea of absolute truth, viewing it as bigotry
The author of Hebrews directly challenges pluralism: God has been leading to Christ all along
Judaism today is a false religion because it denies Christ, the fulfillment of the Old Testament
Anyone who denies Jesus as Messiah worships a false god

outline.title: Application 2: Jesus Is Not Merely a Prophet or Good Man
subpoints: Muslims acknowledge Christ as a prophet but claim Muhammad brought a fuller revelation
Hebrews 1:1-2 directly refutes this: Christ is the revealer of God, not a preliminary step
God is savingly revealed only in Jesus Christ
There is one way to God—exclusively through the Lord Jesus Christ

outline.title: Application 3: No Revelation Exists Outside of Christ
subpoints: Mormonism claims an additional testament and ongoing prophetic revelations
These extra revelations contradict each other and contradict Scripture
Revelation culminates in the Lord Jesus Christ and the New Testament witness to Him
Only the Bible has survived all scrutiny; only Christ has the power to save sinners

outline.title: Conclusion: Our Responsibility Before This Final Revelation
subpoints: Hebrews 2:1 warns: pay careful attention to what we have received, lest we drift away
We possess more revelation than Moses, Abraham, Elijah, or David
We are responsible to read, hear, pray, sing, and live out the Word
Deconstruction typically begins by abandoning the Word and the gathered worship of God

summary:
In this first installment of the series Jesus Christ—The Final Revelation, Pastor Lyro opens with Hebrews 1:1-4 as an introduction to the forthcoming series on the book of Hosea. The sermon establishes how the Old Testament should be read—always through the lens of the cross and the finality of Christ's revelation. The author of Hebrews wrote to Jewish Christians tempted to abandon Christ and return to Judaism, arguing that Christ is the ultimate 'better,' the final and complete word from God.

Pastor Lyro develops the central claim of verses 1-2: God spoke in partial and progressive ways through the Old Testament prophets, but has now spoken finally and fully through His Son. This is not merely a quantitative upgrade in revelation but the eschatological arrival of God's complete self-disclosure. Christ is the goal of all the Old Testament; once we have Christ, we have God's highest word. There is no going back to the incomplete and no going forward to something greater.

Three pastoral applications are drawn. First, in a pluralistic age that rejects absolute truth, believers must hold that Christ is the only truth to which all revelation has pointed. Second, Islam's view that Muhammad surpassed Christ as a revealer of God is a direct contradiction of Hebrews 1. Third, Mormonism's additional revelations and testaments undermine the finality of Christ's revelation; such extra-biblical revelations notoriously contradict each other and are not trustworthy.

The sermon closes with the warning of Hebrews 2:1: since we have been given more revelation than any saint in the Old Testament—more than Moses, David, or Elijah—we are under a greater obligation to take heed. The response called for is careful attention to God's Word in every form: personal reading, corporate worship, meditating, praying, and living out the Scriptures.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 1:1-4

If you're able to remain standing for the reading of God's Word, thank you. We're going to be reading Hebrews chapter 1, verses 1 through 4. The sermon this afternoon is going to be on verses 1 and 2.

God who at various times and in various ways spoke in time past to the fathers by the prophets has in these last days spoken to us by his Son, whom he has appointed heir of all things, through whom also he made the worlds, who being the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person, and upholding all things by the word of his power, when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on High, having become so much better than the angels as he has by inheritance obtained a more excellent name than they.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father, we pray that once again you speak to us. We thank you for your grace in which you reveal yourself to us through your Word. Show yourself to us, we're asking in Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction

This morning's sermon, this afternoon's sermon and next Sunday afternoon's sermon—originally when I was designing the series on Hosea, I had intended these to be one sermon to introduce the series, but it kept on growing and growing and growing and now it's a little over three sermons.

As we saw this morning it is impossible to know who exactly wrote this epistle to the Hebrews. But by looking at the content of the letter itself, we find out that the author of Hebrews was a Bible-saturated, brethren-loving, word-applying, Greek-speaking pastor who loved his congregation. He wrote this letter sometime before AD 70 with the purpose of encouraging a group of Jewish Christians who were considering abandoning Jesus Christ to persevere in the faith.

They had made a profession of faith in Christ, were walking in Christ, and yet there were some of them who wondered if maybe what they had left in Judaism was a little better than what they had come to. The author of Hebrews writes to them, compelling them to think about the supremacy of Christ and the sufficiency of Christ as the antidote to their temptation not to persevere.

## God's Final Word Is Supremely Authoritative

The point for this afternoon is this: God's final word is supremely authoritative.

Look at verse 1 and the first part of verse 2: God who at various times and in various ways spoken in time past to the fathers by the prophets has in these last days spoken to us by his Son. Notice that it's not simply that God has given us a greater revelation than the Old Testament, though he has. It is that he has given us a final revelation in the Lord Jesus Christ. God is saying here, my word was not completely uttered until Christ came. When Christ came, the word spoken in him was my final word.

F.F. Bruce, a British New Testament scholar concerning this passage says, the story of divine revelation is a story of progression up to Christ. But there is no progression beyond him. We move to Christ and then we never move away from him.

It's exactly what Christ is doing in Luke chapter 24. Remember when he met those two guys on the way to Emmaus? He says that starting with Moses and the prophets he explained how all these things pointed to him. Once you get Christ, you've gotten to the very highest point of God's revelation. There's nothing beyond Christ which is greater or higher. So if that's the case, why go back to a time of incomplete revelation?

The words "in these last days" in verse 2 refer to the end times. The New Testament says that we live in the end times already. This time that we live is the last time. There's nothing else that happens. Jesus already came. He's already risen. The author is saying that God spoke a long time ago to the fathers, but now once for all at the end of the ages he has spoken through his Son.

John Calvin says in comment on this passage, just as the Spirit of God in this passage invites all to come as far as Christ, so he forbids them to overstep this last word. The Spirit is inviting: come to Christ, but don't go beyond Christ.

## Three Applications

### Application 1: We Live in a Pluralistic Age

We live in a pluralistic age where people say there's no truth with a capital T. There are lots of truths and they all may be self-contradictory and supplementary at the same time. Our culture doesn't like the idea of someone saying that all truth is found in the Lord Jesus Christ. But we need to understand that is exactly what the author is saying. He's not saying that Christ is a truth. He's saying that Christ is the truth that God has been leading up to all along as he revealed himself in the Old Testament.

The corollary, the attached truth to this truth is that Judaism today is a false religion. Jews today worship a false God. They do not worship the God of the Bible, because the God of the Bible is only worshiped through the Lord Jesus Christ. Anyone that denies Jesus Christ as Messiah is worshiping a false God. No matter what claims they make concerning the Old Testament, because there is no Old Testament religion to go back to. Those who believe in the Old Testament God will believe in the Lord Jesus Christ. It is that simple.

### Application 2: Jesus Is Not Just a Great Prophet or Good Man

Muslims believe that Christ was a godly man and a great prophet. They believe that Christ as a prophet revealed God, but there was a greater revelation of God than the revelation given in Christ—the revelation given to Muhammad. So if you think that Jesus is a godly man, a prophet, a good teacher, and that's all there is, you know different than a Muslim.

But that's not what the Bible teaches concerning Jesus. These verses directly challenge this view. These verses say it's not enough to acknowledge the Lord Jesus as a great prophet. He is the revealer of God. Not Muhammad, but Christ. In no one else is God savingly revealed other than in the Lord Jesus Christ. There's not too many ways to God. There's only one way, and exclusively it is the Lord Jesus Christ.

### Application 3: No Revelation Outside of Christ

This is a direct challenge to other faiths like the Mormon faith who believe that there was some other revelation. Mormons want us to read another testament of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. Apparently the revelation given by the Lord Jesus Christ and through his apostles is not sufficient. So we need another testament. That's somehow the Bible must be supplemented by these other revelations.

There was an authoritative revelation in the Mormon Church that the mark of being cursed by God was being black. It's as times change and then there was another official revelation that being black was no longer a curse from God. Both supposedly authoritative revelations of Jesus Christ—and they contradict each other. That's the kind of revelation we're talking about when we're looking for a revelation outside of the Lord Jesus Christ. This passage directly challenges that view.

## Why the Canon Matters

Now why, as we finish this afternoon, is this idea that the canonical books of the Bible as the revelation of Jesus Christ are the ultimate revelation of God important? The Bible claims to be so. To deny this teaching is to reject the Bible. The Bible also points to Christ while other so-called revelations point to someone else—the Koran to Muhammad, the Book of Mormon to Joseph Smith. Only Christ has the power to save sinners who are dead in their trespasses and sins. Only the Bible has survived all the scrutiny brought to it.

So brothers and sisters, God who at various times and in various ways spoken in time past to the fathers by the prophets, has in these last days spoken to us by his Son. And he says, this is my Son in whom I am well pleased. Listen to him.

## Closing Prayer

Father in heaven, thank you that you are a God who speaks and you speak to us through the Lord Jesus Christ and your Word. Give us a great commitment to that. We pray that this doctrine would be a refreshing doctrine, the knowledge that we know you and there's nothing else that will add to that comforts us. Help us to grasp onto your worthy Word, in Jesus' name, amen.
